description: Labeled accuracy eval set for the Sentinel verifier tiers. 24 self-contained claims about the fictional Aurora Solar Array, 8 per verdict, labeled strictly against the three documents in samples/docs/ (aurora_overview.txt, aurora_operations.txt, aurora_history.md).

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array is located in the Atacama Desert in Chile.
cases.label: SUPPORTED
cases.note: aurora_overview.txt states the plant is a photovoltaic power station located in the Atacama Desert in Chile.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array has an installed capacity of 480 megawatts.
cases.label: SUPPORTED
cases.note: aurora_overview.txt states an installed capacity of 480 megawatts.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array began commercial operation in 2019.
cases.label: SUPPORTED
cases.note: aurora_overview.txt states commercial operation began in 2019.

cases.claim: Self-driving machines wash the Aurora Solar Array's panels after dark to save water.
cases.label: SUPPORTED
cases.note: Paraphrase: aurora_operations.txt says panel cleaning is performed by autonomous robots at night to reduce water consumption.

cases.claim: Condor Energy, the firm that runs the Aurora Solar Array, is based in Santiago.
cases.label: SUPPORTED
cases.note: Paraphrase: aurora_operations.txt says the plant is operated by Condor Energy, headquartered in Santiago.

cases.claim: Building the Aurora Solar Array took roughly three years.
cases.label: SUPPORTED
cases.note: Paraphrase: aurora_history.md says construction started in 2016 and took about three years to complete.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array uses monocrystalline silicon panels.
cases.label: SUPPORTED
cases.note: aurora_history.md states the plant uses fixed-tilt monocrystalline silicon panels.

cases.claim: Money borrowed from the Inter-American Development Bank helped pay for the Aurora Solar Array.
cases.label: SUPPORTED
cases.note: Paraphrase: aurora_history.md says the project was financed partly through a loan from the Inter-American Development Bank.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array has an installed capacity of 750 megawatts.
cases.label: CONTRADICTED
cases.note: Wrong capacity: aurora_overview.txt states 480 megawatts, not 750.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array employs around 400 permanent staff.
cases.label: CONTRADICTED
cases.note: Wrong staff count: aurora_operations.txt states around 90 permanent staff, not 400.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array began commercial operation in 2015.
cases.label: CONTRADICTED
cases.note: Wrong year: aurora_overview.txt states commercial operation began in 2019 (construction only started in 2016).

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array covers roughly 4,000 hectares of desert terrain.
cases.label: CONTRADICTED
cases.note: Wrong area: aurora_overview.txt states roughly 1,100 hectares, not 4,000.

cases.claim: In 2023, the Aurora Solar Array produced approximately 3,500 gigawatt-hours of electricity.
cases.label: CONTRADICTED
cases.note: Wrong output: aurora_operations.txt states approximately 1,200 gigawatt-hours in 2023, not 3,500.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array does not deliver power to the national grid.
cases.label: CONTRADICTED
cases.note: Negation of a stated fact: aurora_overview.txt says power is delivered to the national grid through a dedicated 220 kV line.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array is operated by Andes Power.
cases.label: CONTRADICTED
cases.note: Wrong operator: aurora_operations.txt states the plant is operated by Condor Energy.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array is located in the Mojave Desert in California.
cases.label: CONTRADICTED
cases.note: Wrong location: aurora_overview.txt places the plant in the Atacama Desert in Chile.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array won an international renewable energy award in 2020.
cases.label: UNSUPPORTED
cases.note: Plausible but absent: no document mentions any awards.

cases.claim: The panels at the Aurora Solar Array were manufactured by a company based in China.
cases.label: UNSUPPORTED
cases.note: Plausible but absent: aurora_history.md names the panel type (fixed-tilt monocrystalline silicon) but never the manufacturer.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array has a visitor center that offers guided tours to the public.
cases.label: UNSUPPORTED
cases.note: Plausible but absent: no document mentions a visitor center or tours.

cases.claim: The chief executive of Condor Energy is Maria Reyes.
cases.label: UNSUPPORTED
cases.note: Plausible but absent: aurora_operations.txt describes Condor Energy but never names any executive.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array sells its electricity under a long-term power purchase agreement.
cases.label: UNSUPPORTED
cases.note: Plausible but absent: the documents mention grid delivery but say nothing about how the electricity is sold.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array was built by a consortium of European construction firms.
cases.label: UNSUPPORTED
cases.note: Plausible but absent: aurora_history.md covers financing and schedule but never says who built the plant.

cases.claim: The Aurora Solar Array includes an on-site weather monitoring station.
cases.label: UNSUPPORTED
cases.note: Plausible but absent: no document mentions weather monitoring equipment.

cases.claim: Condor Energy operates several other power plants in Chile.
cases.label: UNSUPPORTED
cases.note: Plausible but absent: the documents only connect Condor Energy to the Aurora Solar Array; its other assets are never mentioned.
